body_txt: An "Easter You thought you would see an easter egg here, but it was I, DIO! Egg" in general parlance is a game played on Easter where kids go out to find as many colored eggs as possible, usually not in very difficult places but are otherwise not obvious if you're not looking for them . When applied to media or other consumer products, it refers to small details that are not easily found or particularly obvious at first but takes a sharp eye or a lot of trial and error to decipher. This includes anything from a reference to something or someone in the production, a Continuity Nod via an article of clothing, Freeze-Frame Bonus , Hidden Tracks on albums, unadvertised DVD Bonus Content , hidden images in audio requiring a spectrogram to see and many others. They're secrets, intended to tickle the fancy of those who discover them. At one point having a hidden scene after the end credits of a movie was considered rare and mischievous, we now call it The Stinger . Interactive media and computer software has broadened the possibilities. Programmers would sneak them in to video games because company policy forbade them from receiving individual credit, sometimes just credit pages to prove authorship to friends. These faded over time as accreditation is more common and for security reasons about malicious programmers inserting undocumented and destructive code note This hasn't stopped programmers from adding them. Sometimes with negative results. And… …the problem of software bugs that can come with black box code. . Developer's Foresight is typically an Easter Egg designed as a reference to how long and grueling it takes for someone to figure it out. This has become basically standard in Video Games , it is expected to fill the game with such things to incite deep exploration to areas only visited with a Rocket Jump , Double Jump or other Advanced Movement Technique . It is the hunt for 100% Completion in a Collection Sidequest , knowing that hours would be spent getting the Last Lousy Point . See? Here's an example of one! Why did you click this anyway? Maybe you should look at the above quote… For time-sensitive Easter Eggs, see Holiday Mode . For in-story Easter Egg dates that reference original air/release dates, see Significant Reference Date . An article on why Easter Eggs exist (focusing on Magic: The Gathering , but applicable to all games) is available here. Related tropes include Bilingual Bonus (when the audience member must know a language other than the main one of the work to get the extra information), Shout-Out (making reference to another work) and Freeze-Frame Bonus (information that passes by too fast to be read at first glance), subtler forms of Foreshadowing (lines and even facial expressions that carry more meaning later on) and What the Hell, Player? (an Easter Egg you get for doing something odd in a game). These are a common "reward" in a Revisit Reward situation. For many, part of the fun of Easter Eggs is discovering them on one's own, so if you're one of those, beware of spoilers .
